coredata.dc:title: Assessing the effects of cellulose-inorganic nanofillers on thermo/pH-dual responsive hydrogels
coredata.dc:creator: Yue, Yiying
Luo, Huiming
Han, Jingquan
Chen, Yiliang
Jiang, Jianchun
coredata.dc:description:
The incorporation of renewable bio-based materials/eco-friendly inorganic nanofillers into stimuli-responsive hydrogels meets the need for biocompatible and non-toxic functional materials. However, it remains challenging to build a three-dimensional network with enhanced mechanical properties and desired stimuli-responsive performances. In this study, dual stimuli-responsive hydrogels with interpenetrating polymer network structure are fabricated by the crosslinking of N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAM) 1
1 NIPAM: N-isopropylacrylamide and sodium alginate (SA) 2
2 SA: Sodium alginate . 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-piperidine-1-oxyl-oxidized cellulose nanofibers (TOCNF) 3
3 TOCNF: 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-piperidine-1-oxyl-oxidized cellulose nanofibers derived from energycane bagasse serves as a carrier material for nanosilicas (NS) 4
4 NS: Nanosilicas and nanoclays (NC) 5
5 NC: Nanoclays and the effects of TOCNF, TOCNF/NS and TOCNF/NC on the hydrogels are thoroughly explored. Among these hydrogels, PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF-NS possesses the largest compressive strength of 66.7 kPa and it is 5.65 times higher than that of PNIPAM/SA. The thermo-sensitivity and pH-sensitivity of hydrogels are evidenced by swelling behaviors, optical properties, contact angle measurements and fluorescence tests. PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF exhibits the largest contact angle and the swelling processes for all the hydrogels are well fitted by pseudo-second-order swelling kinetic model. In addition, after incorporating carbon quantum dots, all the hydrogels display temperature dependent on–off fluorescence properties and this process are fully reversible with the heating and cooling cycles. These novel hydrogels with pH and temperature responsive characteristics and remarkable mechanical properties will open the door for interesting applications in smart sensors etc.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
The incorporation of renewable bio-based materials/eco-friendly inorganic nanofillers into stimuli-responsive hydrogels meets the need for biocompatible and non-toxic functional materials. However, it remains challenging to build a three-dimensional network with enhanced mechanical properties and desired stimuli-responsive performances. In this study, dual stimuli-responsive hydrogels with interpenetrating polymer network structure are fabricated by the crosslinking of N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAM) 1
1 NIPAM: N-isopropylacrylamide and sodium alginate (SA) 2
2 SA: Sodium alginate . 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-piperidine-1-oxyl-oxidized cellulose nanofibers (TOCNF) 3
3 TOCNF: 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-piperidine-1-oxyl-oxidized cellulose nanofibers derived from energycane bagasse serves as a carrier material for nanosilicas (NS) 4
4 NS: Nanosilicas and nanoclays (NC) 5
5 NC: Nanoclays and the effects of TOCNF, TOCNF/NS and TOCNF/NC on the hydrogels are thoroughly explored. Among these hydrogels, PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF-NS possesses the largest compressive strength of 66.7 kPa and it is 5.65 times higher than that of PNIPAM/SA. The thermo-sensitivity and pH-sensitivity of hydrogels are evidenced by swelling behaviors, optical properties, contact angle measurements and fluorescence tests. PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF exhibits the largest contact angle and the swelling processes for all the hydrogels are well fitted by pseudo-second-order swelling kinetic model. In addition, after incorporating carbon quantum dots, all the hydrogels display temperature dependent on–off fluorescence properties and this process are fully reversible with the heating and cooling cycles. These novel hydrogels with pH and temperature responsive characteristics and remarkable mechanical properties will open the door for interesting applications in smart sensors etc.

ce:section.ce:para: Stimuli-responsive hydrogels are three-dimensional polymers whose physical or chemical properties are smartly in response to tiny environmental changes, such as temperature, light, voltage, pH, saline solutions and magnetic fields [1–5]. Recently, thermo-sensitive hydrogels have attracted great attention because temperature is the most common stimuli in daily life. Various agents can be used to synthesize thermo-sensitive hydrogels, such as N-isopropyl acrylamide (NIPAM) [6], propylene glycol [7], N,N-diethylacrylamide [8] and so on. Among them, poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) (PNIPAM) is the most worthwhile and utilized amphiphilic polymers due to its well-defined structure, special properties and low critical solution temperature (LSCT) at 32 °C, which is close to human body temperature. At the LCST, PNIPAM undergoes the phase transition from a swollen hydrated state to a shrunken dehydrated state [9,10]. Therefore, it is promising for drug delivery, oil–water separation, water treatment and smart sensors [11–13].

However, native PNIPAM hydrogels are fragile due to their low polymer density, which limits their practicability. Introducing functional polymer to form smart hydrogels with interpenetrating polymer network (IPN) is a commonly used method to improve its poor mechanical properties and stimulus responsiveness [14]. Sodium alginate, a natural polysaccharide extracted from brown algae, is made up of β-(1,4) linked D-mannuronic acid (M) and α-(1,4)-linked L-guluronic acid (G) with different sequences [15]. The protonation of carboxyl groups on SA gives the hydrogel pH-responsive property. Thus, thermo/pH-dual responsive hydrogels are achieved by incorporating pH-responsive SA into PNIPAM-based networks. The crosslinking of PNIPAM and SA yields a two-step process [16]. The native PNIPAM polymer is covalently crosslinked to form the first network, and then SA chain is combined with the divalent cations (Ca2+, Ba2+) by chelate effect. The obtained hydrogels with IPN structure possess the characteristics of both PNIPAM and SA, such as excellent thermo-responsive, pH-sensitive, and the improved mechanical properties compared with single network composites [9].

Introducing nanofillers to the polymer matrixes is a suitable approach for improving mechanical strength and swelling properties. Among all the nanofillers, cellulose mainly derived from the renewable biomass on earth is a promising candidate due to its superior properties, such as inexhaustible in nature, low cost, sustainability, biodegradability, low density, high aspect ratio and high strength [17,18]. 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-piperidiniyl-1-oxyl (TEMPO)-oxidized cellulose nanofibers (TOCNF) refers to the nanocellulose prepared by oxidation of native cellulose followed by mechanical disintegration, which converts the primary hydroxymethyl groups to carboxyl ones in aqueous media via mechanical agitation [19]. Compared with native cellulose, the TOCNF exhibits good dispersibility in aqueous media owing to the existence of negative charge of –COOH on the surface [20]. Cha et al. showed that the mechanical properties and equilibrium swelling behavior can be substantially improved by incorporation of TOCNF into PNIPAM hydrogels [4] Wei et al. examined the content of TOCNF on the properties of PNIPAM hydrogels. It was also demonstrated that the composite hydrogels exhibited superior swelling abilities and improved compression strength with respect to pure PNIPAM one [5].

What’s more, inorganic nanofillers, such as nanosilicas (NS) [21] and nanoclays (NC) [22], have been widely used in the preparation of hydrogels because of their lightweight, nontoxic and cost effective properties. The incorporation of NS or NC endows the hydrogel the enhanced stiffness, toughness and changed swelling ratio. Morote-Martínez et al. explores that the addition of NS shows striking improvement in mechanical properties of unsaturated polyester resin [23]. Haraguchi et al. reports that NC can act as crosslinker in hydrogels and the resultant hydrogels exhibit extraordinary mechanical strength [24]. Strachota et al. prepared the PNIPAM-NS hydrogel by freezing polymerization and showed that the addition of NS not only changing swelling equilibrium ratio of the hydrogels, but also increases their mechanical properties [25]. Yook et al. examined the effects of magnetically oriented of nontronite NC on polyacrylamide-based hydrogels that are potentially useful for wound dressings. They proved that the degree of swelling anisotropy of the resultant hydrogels varied with the content of the NC loading [26]. The presence of many hydroxyl groups on the surfaces of NS and NC provides strong interfacial interaction with the polymer matrix and TOCNF. After TOCNF/NS or TOCNF/NC incorporated, the swelling behavior of the stimuli-responsive hydrogels would be altered because the water molecules to bond to the sorption sites would be changed with the addition of cellulose-inorganic nanofillers.

Because thermo-responsive hydrogels are frequently utilized to endow the luminescent materials, water-soluble carbon dots (CDs) are synthesized incorporated into hydrogel to integrate with PNIPAM. The CDs were employed as the fluorescent agent due to its high fluorescence quantum yield, fluorescence stability and excitation wavelength dependent properties [27]. Furthermore, the CDs show excellent biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity in contrast to traditional heavy-metal-based quantum dots. Various studies have been carried out to prepare fluorescent polymer materials. Li et al. reported that they designed and synthesized semi-interpenetrating network structured through crosslinking of PNIPAM, CDs and polyacrylamide. The photoluminescence of resulting hydrogels were highly sensitive to external temperature [28]. Zhang et al. introduced that CDs could interacted with PNIPAM matrix via hydrogen bonds, and the LCST of the hydrogels was further determined by fluorescence probe [29].

To the best of our knowledge, although dual thermo- and pH-responsive hydrogels have been conducted, the impacts of synergistic effects of TOCNF and inorganic nanofiller (NS and NC) on the response of smart hydrogels are seldom accessed. This information will be crucial from the consideration of utilizing of PNIPAM/SA hydrogels for targeted applications. Here we report the combination of low cost, lightweight and nontoxic organic/inorganic nanofiller reinforced PNIPAM/SA hydrogels, which can respond to both temperature and pH variation. The effects of TOCNF, TOCNF/NS, TOCNF/NC on the hydrogels were thoroughly explored. The innovative combination of TOCNF and NS/NC generated a plurality of property transitions on stimuli responsiveness, optical properties, mechanical toughness, swelling behavior and stability of hydrogels. The swelling kinetics and the water retention during de-swelling process of hydrogels were investigated to have better insight into the influence of nanofillers on the hydrogels. Fluorescent CDs were also immobilized into these thermo-responsive hydrogels for conducting thermo-controlled fluorescence testing. The resultant hydrogels with dual-stimuli-responsive properties, are promising to be applied in the field of smart sensor, etc.

Energycane bagasse (HO 02–113) was obtained from the Audubon Sugar Institute at Louisiana State University Agricultural Center, St. Gabriel, LA and dried at 50 °C under vacuum. N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAM), sodium alginate (SA), N,N’-methylenebisacrylamide (MBA), potassium persulphate (KPS), 2,2,6,6-Tetramethyl-piperidiniyl-1-oxyl (TEMPO), sodium hypochlorite (NaClO), sodium bromide (NaBr), CaCl2 and N,N,N’,N’-tetramethylethanediamine (TEMED) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Co (Shanghai, China). Nanoclays (NC) and nanosilicas (NS) were purchased from Beijing Deke Island Gold Co (Beijing, China).

Cellulose I fibers isolation were as described in our previous work [30]. 2.0 g of isolated cellulose was introduced to 400 ml deionized water containing 0.033 g TEMPO and 0.33 g NaBr. The mixture was stirred at a speed of 400 r/min. Once 15.0 mmol/g of NaClO solution was involved, the oxidizing process of TOCNF started. During the reaction, the pH value of the mixture was kept at 10.0 by continually adding 0.5 mol/L NaOH solution. Upon completion, the fibers were washed with de-ionized water for several cycles until a neutral pH of 7.0. Then the resultant slurry was processed through ultrasonic homogenizer (JY98-IIIDN, Ningbo, China) for 5.0 min to obtain the slurry of TOCNF. The oxidation schematic of TOCNF is shown in Fig. 1 a. The suspension was kept in the refrigerator at 5 °C before using. A certain amount of suspension weighed m0 was put in an oven until the constant weight mt . The solid content of TOCNF was calculated as mt × 100%/m0 .

The NIPAM/SA-based hydrogels were prepared by in-situ polymerization. The schematic diagram of preparation process was shown in Fig. 1b. 1.2 g NIPAM and 0.1 g SA were dissolved in15.0 g of 0.16 wt% TOCNF aqueous suspensions. The mixture was cooled in an ice bath followed by the incorporating crosslinker MBA and initiator KPS. 20 μL TEMED was then added as accelerator to start the polymerization. The compound was poured into a cylindrical mold with a diameter of 40 mm and settled at 5 °C refrigerator for gelation. After being cross-linked for 2 h, the hydrogels were separated from the mold and washed with deionized water for three times to remove the un-reacted monomers. Finally, the hydrogels were soaked overnight in CaCl2 solution for ionic crosslinking. The prepared hydrogel was designated as NIPAM/SA-TOCNF. The processing conditions of NIPAM/SA-TOCNF-NS and NIPAM/SA-TOCNF-NC were exactly the same as NIPAM/SA-TOCNF except introducing 0.024 g NS or NC, respectively. For comparison, the hydrogel prepared without TOCNF, NS and NC was labeled as NIPAM/SA. NIPAM/SA was synthesized by adding 1.2 g NIPAM and 0.1 g SA in 15 ml water. All other processing conditions were the same as those used for the NIPAM/SA-TOCNF. The sample composition and designations of hydrogels were summarized in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots and PNIPAM-CDs and PNIPAM/SA-CDs based hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: The carbons dots (CDs) were prepared via hydrothermal method using citrate and urea as precursors [31]. Citrate (0.768 g) and urea (0.72 g) were added to 20 ml deionized water under magnetic stirring until a uniform transparent mixture was received. The obtained solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave (50 ml) and heated at 200 °C for 8 h. After cooling naturally, the light yellow in color, clear and transparent solution with impurities was centrifuged for 10 min with the rotating speed of 11,000 r/min and further filtrated by syringe filters (0.22 μm) to remove large particles. Finally, the CDs were stored at 5 °C and kept away from light. In addition, to investigate the thermo-controlled fluorescent performance, PNIPAM/SA-CDs hydrogels were synthesized by substituting deionized water with CDs solution and all the following procedures were exactly the same as Chapter 2.3. In brief, 1.2 g NIPAM and 0.1 g SA were dissolved in 15 ml CDs solution. After introducing MBA, KPS and TEMED following by immersing in CaCl2 solution, the double network structured PNIPAM/SA-CDs hydrogel was prepared. The single network structured hydrogel PNIPAM-CDs was prepared with the same process except involving in SA and CaCl2 solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology of the TOCNF, NS and NC aqueous suspensions and the prepared hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: The TOCNF, NS and NC aqueous suspensions were diluted to 0.001–0.002 wt% and characterized by SEM (JEM-1400, JEOL Ltd. Japan). FESEM (JSM-7600F, JEOL, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 5 kV were used to investigate the morphology of the obtained hydrogels.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The structure of the hybrid hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: FTIR spectra of the hydrogels were recorded using a VERTEX 80v spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. USA) with Attenuated Total Reflectance (ATR) mode. The samples were scanned in the spectral range of 4000–400 cm−1 with a data acquisition of 4 cm−1 per point.

The compression tests were performed by a mechanical testing machine (TY-8000B, Jiangsu, China). The hydrogels specimens were made into cylinder with dimensions of 40 mm in diameter and 10 mm in average thickness. They were compressed with the speed of 5 mm/min and 5.0 kN loading capacity at room temperature.

For the swelling kinetic analysis, hydrogels were cut into small rectangles and dried in an oven for 48 h. Dried hydrogels were left to swell in deionized water at room temperature. The hydrogels were withdrawn from the solution and weighed after wiping the surface water at regular intervals. The swelling ratio (SR) was calculated by the Eq (1).

where m t was the mass of swollen hydrogel at time t, and m 0 was the initial mass of dry hydrogel.

To study the temperature-responsive properties of hydrogels, the dry samples were soaked in deionized water with different temperatures ranging from 5 to 50 °C. Initial weight m 0 and final weight m t as recorded and the excess water was removed by the way introduced above. The swelling ratio was calculated as follows Eq. (2).

The pH-dependent swelling capacities were measured by the swelling of hydrogels at different pH values. HCl and NaOH were added to the de-ionized water to keep the pH at 4.00 and 9.06, respectively. The swelling ratio of the obtained hydrogles under different pH was calculated by Eq. (2) as well. Pre-weighed dry samples m0 were immersed in acid/alkaline solutions for 48 h and the final weight mt was recorded after the removal of excess water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water retention and contact angle measurements
ce:section.ce:para:
Prior to study the water retention of hydrogels, hydrogel pieces were allowed to reach their own equilibrium swelling ratio at 25 °C and weighted as ms . Then, they were placed in deionized water at 50 °C. The mass of hydrogel was weighed every 20 min for a constant weight (mt ). The water retention was calculated by Eq. (3).
(3)

Contact angle measurements were carried out with the contact angle system JC2000D1 (Shanghai, China). A drop of distilled water was deposited on the surface of hydrogels with dimensions Φ40 mm × 5 mm. The drop shape was caught by a digital camera until it stayed still at 25 °C. After soaking in 60 °C hot water, the contact angle was also recorded to study temperature-dependent behavior.

The fluorescent emission spectra were recorded on a PE LS55 spectrophotometer over the wavelength range of 300–700 nm. The wavelength from 350 to 450 nm was selected to excite the fluorescence. For temperature-sensitive cycle tests, the hydrogels were heated at 50 °C and cooled at an ice bath.

PNIPAM/SA hydrogels were synthesized via in-situ polymerization and the reaction mechanism is displayed in Fig. 2 a. Firstly, the covalent crosslinking of PNIPAM with MBA was induced using KPS and TEMED as the initiator and accelerator, respectively (Fig. 2b). Then, SA reacted with ionic group Ca2+, and the crosslinked SA and Ca2+ interacted with the first network to form the IPN structure (Fig. 2c). TOCNF, TOCNF/NS and TOCNF/NC were incorporated into the hydrogel through hydrogen bonding (Fig. 2d). For instance, there was a hydrogen bonding interaction between the carboxyl groups in SA and the amide groups in PNIPAM chains.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dispersion state of nanofillers and microstructure of hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 3 a–c, NS particles were spheroidal and NCs were flaky, while TOCNF presented wire-like structure with nano-scale diameter. The diameter distributions of NS, NC and TOCNF were analyzed and plotted as histograms based on the TEM images. They were well fitted by Guassian distribution (R2 = 0.9993, 0.9733, 0.9864) in Fig. 3d–f. The parameters of their own nonlinear curve fitting equations are listed in Fig. 3g. The parameters of Amplitude version of Gaussian curve fitting equations are listed in Fig. 3g (the meanings of Y0, Xc, A, ω are offset, peak center, amplitude and width, respectively). Generally, NS and NC particles with a size of 26.5 ± 5.8 nm and 28.5 ± 10.8 nm were aggregated to form a multilayer agglomerates (Fig. 3a, b). As shown in Fig. 3c, the TOCNF fibers with an average length of 486.1 ± 193.4 nm were intertwined to form a stably web-like structure. Owing to the additive electrostatic force among carboxyl groups, a good dispersion of TOCNF was observed. The degree of oxidation of TOCNF is presented in Fig. S1.

The morphology of the hydrogels after the lyophilization is presented in Fig. 3h. A micro-level and irregular porous structure was clearly observed for all the freeze dried hydrogels. Owing to the low loading level of the nano-fillers, no striking difference in microstructure among PNIPAM/SA, PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF, PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF-NS and PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF-NC was observed. Thus, only the image of freeze-dried PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF-NS is presented in Fig. 3h. Fig. 3i shows the solid and transparent appearance of PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF-NS hydrogel. After the lyophilization process, PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF-NS hydrogel with Interpenetrating Polymer Network (IPN) structure displayed a large macro-porous structure with an average pore diameter of 306 ± 101 μm (Fig. 3h), whereas the PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF-NS hydrogel without soaking in CaCl2 possessed uniform micro-porous structure with a mean pore diameter of 76 ± 40 μm (Fig. 3j). Additionally, compared with the mean cell wall thickness of 1.0 ± 0.36 μm for PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF-NS hydrogel without further crosslinking with Ca2+ (Fig. 3j), the IPN structured freeze-dried hydrogels had compact walls and they were ten to sixty times thicker. The enlarged pore diameter and thickened cell wall confirmed the further ionic crosslinking between SA and Ca2+. This result is in accordance with the previous study [25]. In addition, the transparent of PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF-NS in Fig. 3i and transmittance of the hydrogels in Fig. S2 confirmed the well dispersion of NS particles in the NIPAM and SA matrixes without evident phase separation, indicating TOCNF acted as an ideal dispersant to carry NS well-dispersing in PNIPAM/SA matrixes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical structure hydrogels determined from FTIR analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 a presents the FTIR spectra of NIPAM, SA, TOCNF, NS and NC within the range of 4000–400 cm−1. In the spectrum of NIPAM, the asymmetrical and symmetrical stretching vibrations of the N–H gave rise to the overlapping bands at 3260 and 3350 cm−1 [32]. The characteristic absorption bands appearing at 1624 and 1550 cm−1 was assigned to the C = O stretching on amide I and the N–H bending on amide II [33]. The absorption band at 2970 and 2935 cm−1 in this spectrum was indicative of the asymmetric and symmetric vibration bands of methyl group [34]. And the band at 1388 and 1460 cm−1 depicted the antisymmetric stretching of the carbonyl groups and the methylene scissoring [35]. For SA, the peaks at 3420 cm−1, 2917 cm−1, 1026 cm−1 and 810 cm−1 were responsible for the O–H, the C–H, the C-O-C and the Na-O stretching, respectively [14,36]. The characteristic bands of 1620 cm−1 and 1412 cm−1 were mainly due to asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of carboxyl group [15]. In the TOCNF curves, the characteristic peaks at 3350, 2894, 1313 and 1026 cm−1 confirmed the presence of the O–H stretching, the C–H stretching, the –CH2 scissoring and the pyranose ring skeletal vibration, respectively [37]. The characteristic peaks at 1600 and 1406 cm−1 were attributed to the carboxyl groups, confirming the effective in converting hydroxyl groups into carboxyl ones on C6. When it came to NS and NC, the broad band at 3420 cm−1 implied the –OH stretching vibration of interlayer water [38]. The absorption at 1105 cm−1 was arose from the asymmetric vibration of the Si-O-Si and the peaks at 958 cm−1 corresponded to the Si-OH functional groups in NS spectrum [35,39]. Meanwhile, the overlapping between 590 and 820 cm−1 in NC spectra were ascribed to some oxide like the Al-O and the Si-O [40].

Fig. 4b displays the spectra of polymerized hydrogels. The vibration of the –COOH in alginate chains at 1620 were shifted to 1633 in PNIPAM/SA, suggesting the crosslinking of G units through Ca2+ cation. The broad band at 3420 cm−1 assigning to the stretching vibration peak of the –OH group was overlapped with the peak at 3250 cm−1 corresponding to stretching vibrations of the N–H in all the spectra. This was a clear sign for the formation of the intermolecular H-N···H-O bonds between SA and PNIPAM. The developing of hydrogen bonds between SA and PNIPAM cm−1 could be further evidence by small peaks shifting from 3260 and 1550 cm−1 in NIPAM to 3250 to 1545 cm−1 in PNIPAM/SA [12]. After the incorporation of TOCNF, the intensities of the –OH stretching at 3420 cm−1 in PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF, PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF-NS, and PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF-NC were greater than those in PNIPAM/SA, implying that TOCNF generated new intermolecular hydrogen bonds in PNIPAM/SA matrixes [37]. In addition, a slight increasing intensities at 1105 cm−1 and around 550 cm−1 which were arising from the asymmetric vibration of the Si-O-Si and the Si-O were shown in PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF-NS spectrum, suggesting the intertwining between NS and PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF [41]. Compared with NS, the intensities of NC spectrum was relatively lower owing to its tendency to agglomerate. This reduction of reactive groups resulted in the decreasing in reactivity for PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF-NC. Owing to the low contents of TOCNF, NS and NC, most of the characteristic bands were overwhelmed by those peaks associated with PNIPAM/SA matrixes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical properties of hydrogels under compression
ce:section.ce:para: The compression stress–strain curves of the hydrogels are shown in Fig. 5 a. The compressive stress of all the hydrogels increased slowly with the strain in the first stage (<20% initial strain). This linear stress–strain behavior suggested that the hydrogels followed Hooke’s law. In this stage, all of the hydrogels were in elastic state and could be return to their initial length once the stress was removed. As the stain increased to 20%–60%, the stress presented a sharp increasing, demonstrating the energy-dissipation occurred inside the hydrogel to resist the compression stress. It was reported that the slopes of the linear segment of the stress–strain curves were numerically equal to the elastic modulus of hydrogels [42]. The elastic modulus of PNIPAM/SA was 0.075 ± 0.007 kPa, while PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF, PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF-NS and PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF-NC were 0.25 ± 0.018, 0.92 ± 0.049 and 0.30 ± 0.089 kPa, which were almost 3.3, 12.2 and 4.0 times higher than those of NIPAM/SA, respectively (Fig. 5b). For PNIPAM/SA, compressive strength reached 19.8 kPa at the strain of 48%, which was much higher than that of PNIPAM hydrogel with SIPN structure reported by Wei [32]. The results indicated that the intertwining of SA-Ca2+ with the PNIPAM network could effectively increase the physical crosslinking density and reduce the mobility of crosslink points, leading to a more compact and tight structure [43]. After incorporating of TOCNF, PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF showed remarkable enhancement in strength. The possible reason was the well dispersed TOCNF could crosslink with each other and participate in the crosslinking of PNIPAM and SA through hydrogen bonding, resulting in the increasing of density (Fig S3). Consequently, the homogenously dispersed TOCNF (Fig S2) could sustain a portion of the compressive load. Among all of these hydrogels, PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF-NS showed the most distinguished mechanical strength, indicating NS also had reinforcing effects on the hydrogels. The formation of hydrogen bonding between NS and PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF could help transfer the applied load from matrix to NS. As a result, the possible sliding among polymer matrixes was tremendously impeded [44]. Compared with PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF-NC, the outstanding enhancement of NS particles were possibly attributed to the better dispersion and fewer agglomerations than NC particles [45]. The inserted picture in Fig. 5a exhibited that a cylindrical NIPAM/SA-TOCNF-NC hydrogel could undergo about 1.0 kg weight, which was almost 56 times larger than its own weight. As shown in Fig. 6 b, the compressive strength of PNIPAM/SA, PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF, PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF-NS and PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF-NC were 11.8 ± 2.1, 30.0 ± 6.9, 66.7 ± 8.1 and 37.3 ± 0.49 kPa at the strain of 35%, respectively, which was consistent with the trend of the elastic modulus of the prepared hydrogels. The energy absorption-strain curves of the hydrogels are shown in Fig. 5c. The tendencies of energy absorption were the same as the compressive properties of hydrogels. These results also confirmed that the addition of 0.024 g of TOCNF, NS and NC particles could effectively assist energy distribution and dissipation [46].

The results of five-cyclic compression testing with maximum strains of 40% for prepared hydrogels are shown in Fig. 5d. In the first loading, PNIPAM/SA based hydrogels could endure up to 40% compression and recover to their initial shape after releasing the load, suggesting their outstanding elasticity and toughness. The unloading paths of all the hydrogels in this loading displayed a hysteresis loop with a residual strain. All the following successive loading–unloading cycles went through the same procedure as the first one and an evident overlapping was observed for the next four cycles. The possible reason was that the inner loosely bound free water was squeezed out of the gels and could not come back before the following cycle [47]. After five cycles, no visible fracture of hydrogels was observed as shown in Fig. 5d, indicating the tough hydrogels could endure cyclic loading–unloading compression with negligibly permanent deformation. After incorporating TOCNF, NS and NC, the mechanical stresses of PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF, PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF-NS, PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF-NC hydrogels reached to 42, 85 and 75 kPa, which were the 2.2, 4.5 and 3.9 times higher than that of PNIPAM/SA, respectively, suggesting the essential role of nanofillers [46].

As we know, PNIPAM chains consist of hydrophobic groups (isopropyl-methy) and hydrophilic groups (amide). These two parts cooperating together led to a sharp and reversible phase transition at the LCST [10,43]. The schematic of phase and volume transformation for PNIPAM hydrogels is shown in Fig. 6a. When the temperature was below the LCST, the surface of hydrogels was hydrophilic because amide groups on NIPAM chains could easily interact with water molecules through hydrogen bonds, which helped their affinity toward water molecules. The hydrogels showed a translucent appearance like the left picture in Fig. 6f. In contrast, when the temperature was above the LCST, the phase and volume transformation happened and the hydrogen bonds were destroyed [48]. As shown in Fig. 6b, PNIPAM acted as hydrophobic ones and aggregate quickly. As the squeezing out of the associated water, the hydrogels turned to be opaque as the right picture in Fig. 6f.

The equilibrium swelling ratio (ESR) is one of the most important parameter to describe thermo-responsive hydrogels. The ESR is highly related to the nature of the polymer matrix, the degree of crosslinking and the elasticity of the polymeric network [49]. Fig. 6c displays the variation of ESR values of these hydrogels at different temperatures. All the hydrogels exhibited similar thermo-responsive profiles with a sharp decrease between 30 and 35 °C, indicating their LCST lied in this temperature range. Under the LCST, all the hybrid hydrogels with TOCNF, NS and NC were expected to improve the swelling ratios owing to the hydrophilic nature of these matters, which was beneficial to the water adsorption. However, the ESR values of these hydrogels were in the order of PNIPAM/SA > PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF-NS > PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF-NC > PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF. These phenomena indicated that, when TOCNF with the carboxyl content of 1.132 mmol/g (Fig. S1) was incorporated into the networks, both the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on TOCNF could form hydrogen bonds with amide groups on PNIPAM, resulting in the reduction of affinity toward water molecules. Therefore, the hydration role of amide groups was weakened, followed by the decreasing of swelling ratios [32]. Furthermore, when NS and NC particles were dispersed in the PNIPAM/SA matrixes, the –OH and Si-OH groups could interacted with water molecules by hydrogen bonds. Thus the network could maintain more water molecules, leading to a higher ESR. Compared with NC, NS with smaller particle size was beneficial to the homogeneous dispersion in the polymer matrix, resulting in the larger ESR for PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF-NS than PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF-NC. On the contrary, when the temperature was above the LCST, the isopropyl-methy groups of PNIPAM were exposed and the polymer chains were collapsed and aggregated, leading to a dense opaque state of hydrogels. The ESR behaviors of all the proposed hydrogels were similar, indicating that the corporation of 0.0024 g TOCNF, NS and NC did not cause an obvious hydrophilic tendency when the temperature was above the LCST.

Water contact angle was another important parameter for thermo-sensitive properties [50]. The water contact angle measurements and the enlarged pictures of water drops are displayed in Fig. 6d and e. The contact angles of PNIPAM/SA, PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF, PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF-NS and PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF-NC were 37.8 ± 4.1°, 55.6 ± 3.1°, 46.2 ± 4.6° and 54.2 ± 4.7° at 25 °C, respectively. After immersed in the 50 °C water, their contact angles were increased to 93.5 ± 6.4°, 95.8 ± 8.2°, 94.3 ± 7.4° and 95.4 ± 12.4°, demonstrating a thermo-responsive property. The rising of contact angles was ascribable to the increasing of hydrogel surface tension causing by the transformation of PNIPAM from hydrophilicity to hydrophobicity. When the temperature was below the LCST, PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF displayed a larger contact angle than PNIPAM/SA. The possible reason was that the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on TOCNF could interact with amide groups on PNIPPM to form hydrogen-bonds, and thus the association role of hydrogels with water was weakened. With further incorporation of hydrophilic NS/NC, the total number of hydroxyl groups and carboxyl groups in hydrogels was greater than that of amide groups. Then the existence of abundant hydroxyl and carboxyl groups enhanced the hydrophilic of hydrogels, resulting in a slight decrease in contact angle [32]. In addition, NC generating a larger contact angle than NS might relate to its tendency towards aggregation. Therefore, compared with NS, NC could provide fewer hydroxyl groups. When the temperature was above the LCST, the contact angles of all of the hydrogels increased due to the structures changes of the PNIPAM on the surface of hydrogels. It also can be seen that the values of contact angles of all the hydrogels were basically the same, indicating there was no significant change in the surface properties of hydrogels after incorporation of small amount of hydrophilic TOCNF, NS and NC at 50 °C.

Swelling kinetics of hydrogels was investigated to have better insight into the influence of nanofillers on the hydrogels. In Fig. 7 a, the swelling kinetic curves for all the hydrogels followed the same trend at 25 °C. The swelling ratio increased quickly in the initial stage (<400 min), and then followed with a slower swelling rate until equilibrium. Compared with the other hydrogels, the neat hydrogel absorbed less water at the beginning of swelling process, because the less accessible hydroxyl groups resulted in the reduction of interactions with water molecules [9]. The incorporation of TOCNF, NS and NC increased the contact surface area and the swelling rates in the initial stage. As swelling process gradually approached to equilibrium, PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF based hydrogel possessed a low swelling ratio owning to amide groups on PNIPAM were interacted with carboxyl or hydroxyl groups on TOCNF. As a result, the association of the amide groups on PNIPAM with water molecules was declined. Because NS with larger surface area had more accessible hydroxyl groups in comparison with those on NC, the swelling ratio of PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF-NS was larger than that of PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF-NC. To evaluate swelling kinetics of the prepared hydrogels, the data were assessed by pseudo-second-order swelling kinetic model:
(4)

The plots of

against t for hydrogels were well fitted by linear equation (Fig. 7b), suggesting that the total swelling process agree with pseudo-second-order swelling kinetic model. In the equation above, W t represented the amount of absorbed water at time t and the constant A and B could be obtained from the intercept and slope of the plotted straight lines. The swelling rate constant KS and the theoretical equilibrium swelling capacity W∞ were calculated according to the Eqs. (5) and (6) [51]. The experimental equilibrium swelling capacity Weq and theoretical values obtained by these equations are listed in the Table 2 .

It can be seen that the theoretical equilibrium swelling capacity W ∞ was close to the experimental values Weq . Furthermore, after incorporating TOCNF, NS and NC, the swelling rate KS was higher than that of neat hydrogels, suggesting the hydrophilicity nature of these nanofillers.

It was believed that the initial swelling process was well fitted by Eq. (7). In order to verify the diffusion mechanism of the hydrogels, the swelling data in the initial stage of hydrogels (<400 min) was fitted with Eq. (7).
(7)

K w
t

where Wt represented the amount of absorbed water at time t and W∞ meant the amount at equilibrium swollen state, respectively. And K w was the characteristic constant related to the structure of hydrogel. Additionally, n was the diffusion exponent, which related to the transport mechanism of water. Normally, when n varied from 0 to 0.5, the swelling process could be predicted by the Fickian diffusion mechanism. If not, relaxation of polymer chains played the dominant role and the swelling process followed non-Fickian diffusion mechanism [52].

The values of K w and n are listed in Table 2. The fitted values in the initial stage and whole swelling process are presented in Fig. 7c and d, respectively. It is clearly that the initial swelling process was fitted by Eq. (7), whereas this mechanism not fitted for the entire swelling process. In addition, the value of diffusion exponent n for PNIPAM/SA was 0.656. This indicated the swelling process of PNIPAM/SA was matched with non-Fickian diffusion mechanism. Inversely, diffusion exponent n of other hydrogels within 0–0.5 obeyed Fickian diffusion mechanism. These conclusions were accordance with the previous result that the swelling process was highly related to the infiltration of water molecules into hydrogel networks [53].

The water retention during de-swelling process of hydrogels is shown in Fig. 7e. All the hydrogels started to shrink immediately once they were immersed in the hot water. Among all the hydrogels, PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF-NS presented the lowest response rates. For instance, within 25 min, 30% of the water adsorbed in PNIPAM/SA was expelled, while PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF-NS lost only 20% of the water. The probable reason might be the incorporation of NS into the PNIPAM/SA network resulting in stronger interactions with water molecules. After 200 min, a majority of water was squeezed out and the process reached to the equilibrium for all the hydrogels. The reduction of de-swelling rates was attributed to the formation of a strong, thick and dense hydrophobic skin as the increasing of temperature. This skin layer gradually closed the water channel to prevent water loss [54].

The effect of pH on the swelling properties of PNIPAM/SA based hydrogels was also investigated at 25 °C (Fig. 7f). It was obvious that all of the hydrogels had higher swelling ratios at pH = 7.0 compared with the other pH values studied. NIPAM chains have been proved not to be pH-sensitive. Both of SA and TOCNF had abundant carboxyl groups in molecular chain, and the transformation of carboxyl group into the ionized form (–COO–) was highly relied on the pH values, thus SA and TOCNF was the key to pH-dependent swelling behavior.

The low swelling ratios at pH = 1 were mainly ascribed to the protonation of –COO– groups on SA and TOCNF. This protonation could greatly trigger the formation of a strong hydrogen bond between –COOH in SA and –CONH- groups in NIPAM, resulting in the hindering of the polymer chain relaxation in hydrogel. It is reported that the pKa values of the guluronic and mannuronic acid from SA were 3.38 and 3.65, respectively [9]. At pH higher than the pKa values of the guluronic and mannuronic acid (e.g. pH = 4.0), the –COOH groups were partly ionized and the increase of the swelling ratio was caused by the increase in electrostatic repulsion [55]. At pH = 7.0, there is a maximum swelling ratio of all the hydrogels, which may be attributed to the highest degree of dissociation of –COOH groups. Beyond this pH value, for instance at pH = 9.0, the additional cation for adjusting pH (e.g. Na+) could effectively prevent the electrostatic repulsion among –COO– groups in SA and TOCNF chains, resulting in a decrease in SR [9,56]. At pH = 12.0, the values of swelling ratios decreased rapidly. This phenomenon was attributed to that excessive of Na+ shielded the –COO– anions and the hydrogen bonding interactions were partly disrupted in strongly alkaline environment [55].

Among most of the tests conducting in acidic and alkaline solutions, the swelling ratios of hydrogels followed the trend of PNIPAM/SA > PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF-NS > PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF-NC > PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF. Compared with PNIPAM/SA, PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF had lower swelling properties because TOCNF offered more –COOH groups to involve in hydrogen bonding in acidic media. Under alkaline conditions, TOCNF with more –COOH groups required an excessive NaOH to achieve the desired pH. Because of the stronger shielding effect of additional Na+, the swelling ratio of PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF was lower than that of PNIPAM/SA. Compared with PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF, PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF-NC and PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF-NS hydrogels exhibited greater swelling capabilities due to the hydrophilic –OH groups could interact with more water molecule by hydrogen bonding. Owing to NS possessed the smaller particle size and the greater number of exposed functional groups than NC at the same mass, the swelling ratio of PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF-NS was larger than that of PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF-NC. At pH = 12.0, all the hydrogels exhibited the similar swelling behavior. A possible reason was that the shielding effect of Na+ was the dominant reason for the decreasing of the swelling ratio of the hydrogels and this effective screening effect leads to the weakening of the distinctions among all the hydrogels.

To further explore the thermo-sensitivity of the hydrogels, CDs were employed to study the temperature dependent on–off fluorescence properties. Fig. 8 a exhibits fluorescent emission spectra of CDs solution with 10-time dilution under different excitation wavelengths. Obviously, the CDs could be excited at a wide range (from 350 nm to 450 nm). This excitation-dependent behavior was related to surface defects and the wide-ranging sizes of CDs [57]. The maximum emission intensity was obtained when excited at 380 nm. In Fig. 8b, when with dilution ratio ranged from 1 to 20 fold, the fluorescence intensity of CDs gradually increased from 25 to the 580 a.u. When diluted with more water, the fluorescence intensity decreased with a slight blue shift. In Fig. 8c, the cuvette containing diluted CDs under irradiation with 350 nm UV lamp displayed bright blue while the non-diluted solution appeared dull in color. Therefore, the optimal excitation wavelength (380 nm) and dilution ratio (20 times) were employed for further tests.

In Fig. 8d, with increasing in temperature, the fluorescent intensity of CDs solution slightly declined due to the increasing probability of agglomeration. Compared with the pure CDs, PNIPAM/SA-CDs exhibited the declining fluorescence intensity due to the shading of hydrogel matrixes. In addition, PNIPAM/SA-CDs had a sharp reduction in fluorescence intensity from 122a.u. at 30 °C to 48a.u. at 35 °C (Fig. 8e). This was caused by the sharply decreasing in light absorbance arising from thermo-triggered phase and volume transformation of the PNIPPM. Similar results have been previously reported [13,58]. When the temperature was below the LCST, CDs were uniformly distributed in PNIPAM/SA matrix by hydrogen-bonding interactions among carboxyl or hydroxyl groups on CDs and SA as well as amide groups on PNIPAM chains [59]. The transparent PNIPAM/SA-CDs hydrogels allowed light to pass through and thus excited the high fluorescent intensity under UV lamp; whereas when the temperature was above the LCST, the hydrogels exhibited the white solid state. The reasons for these observations were the hydrophilic amide groups shelled by the hydrophobic isopropyl-methyl groups along with the self-absorption of fluorescence and aggregation of CDs, leading to high reflection and scattering of light [29].

PNIPAM/SA-CDs and three kinds of PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF-CDs based hydrogels were subjected to 10 heating–cooling processes in Fig. 8f. The reversible fluorescent turning on and off could be achieved by the stimuli of temperature. The average intensities of PNIPAM/SA-CDs, PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF-CDs, PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF-NS-CDs and PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF-NC-CDs were 129, 121, 110, 89 a.u. at 0 °C and 42, 36, 24, 14 a.u. at 50 °C, respectively, which was in agreement with the results observed in transmittance tests (Fig. S2). Hence, the obtained hydrogels could response to the change in temperature instantly. The excellent characteristics of the hydrogels were highly expected to open the door for interesting applications in thermo sensors.

In this study, a series PNIPAM/SA based hydrogels were synthesized through homogeneously dispersing TOCNF into the IPN structured hydrogels. TOCNF derived from energycane bagasse acted as a dispersant to carry two inorganic nanofillers – NS and NC to well-disperse in PNIPAM/SA matrixes. The obtained hydrogels had a macro-porous structure with a high water content of ~87% and a low density of ~1.16 g/cm3. The low content of TOCNF, NS and NC (accounting for 2% of the NIPAM) served as nano-reinforcing agents to improve the compressive strength and elastic modulus. Among them, compressive strength and modulus of PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF-NS reached 66.7 ± 8.1 kPa at the strain of 35% and 0.92 ± 0.049 kPa, which was 5.65 and 12.2 times higher than PNIPAM/SA, respectively. The innovative combination of TOCNF and NS/NC generated a desired temperature/pH-responsive performance. PNIPAM/SA showed the highest swelling ratio, which was 1.38 fold than that of PNIPAM/SA-TOCNF and the swelling procedure was well fitted by pseudo-second-order swelling kinetic model. Carbon dots were employed for detection of temperature variations and the composite hydrogels exhibited reversible temperature dependent on–off fluorescence properties. Therefore, the developed hydrogels with improved mechanical properties and stimuli-sensitivity have great potential for on-demand in temperature/pH sensors.
